part: A Boy Adrift on the Upper West Side
title: Skipped Grades and a Regents Scholarship
summary: Interviewed in Brooklyn on March 16, 2013, by John Dittmer, Dr. H. Jack Geiger opens on his Upper West Side upbringing as the son of a doctor and a microbiologist mother, being skipped four or five grades, and graduating at fourteen from Townsend Harris, a magnet high school taught by City College faculty who taught him about the colonial rape of Africa before being fired under the Rapp-Coudert Act.

part: A Boy Adrift on the Upper West Side
title: Billie Holiday on 52nd Street
summary: Too young at fourteen for any college to admit him, Geiger describes hanging out on 52nd Street listening to Billie Holiday and Lester Young, working as a copyboy for the New York Times, and being so moved by Canada Lee in Orson Welles's Native Son that he talked his way backstage and began visiting the actor.

part: A Boy Adrift on the Upper West Side
title: A Couch on Edgecombe Avenue
summary: Geiger recounts the worsening conflict with his European-born immigrant parents, packing a suitcase one Sunday night and going to Canada Lee's penthouse at 155th and Edgecombe in Harlem, where Lee let him sleep on the couch and gave him an education sitting in the corner as Paul Robeson, Langston Hughes, and Adam Clayton Powell Jr. came through.

title: This Was Not a Race Riot
topic: The Harlem Uprising and Canada Lee
summary: Geiger describes a Harlem ablaze in 1941 with stories of black soldiers abused at southern camps and the 1943 uprising sparked by a rumor that military police shot a black soldier, a selective trashing of white-owned 125th Street stores rude to black customers, and how the FBI threatened Canada Lee with blacklisting if he would not denounce Paul Robeson, which Lee refused.

title: From Jockey to Actor
summary: Geiger recounts Canada Lee's blacklisting after he refused to denounce Robeson, and Lee's life story: a Jamaican family, a violin prodigy turned jockey turned light-heavyweight contender ended by a retinal detachment, who wandered into Welles's Mercury Theatre in Harlem and became a gifted actor, starring in the Broadway Native Son.

title: An Evening With Langston Hughes
summary: Geiger recalls his middle-class white parents bravely attending a black party at Lee's penthouse, his mother spending two hours cooking in the kitchen with a man she never recognized as Langston Hughes, and the era's ritual of alerting the doorman so a black dinner guest would be let in, a transformative window into Harlem and its daily struggles.

title: Picketing the Raytheon Plant
summary: Admitted at fifteen to the University of Wisconsin in 1941, Geiger describes getting a 1942 letter from Bayard Rustin and A. Philip Randolph to organize for the first threatened March on Washington, picketing a Madison defense plant that would not hire blacks, an effort that pressured Franklin Roosevelt into an executive order barring discrimination in defense hiring and triggering a new wave of the Great Migration.

title: Off the University's Housing List
summary: Geiger recounts his first structured campaign at Wisconsin, forcing the university to drop approved off-campus landlords who refused Jews, blacks, and Asians, then meeting James Farmer in Chicago, helping start one of the earliest CORE chapters in Madison, and working a police beat at sixteen as the only reporter who needed a special pass to be on the street after the wartime youth curfew.

title: The Only Integrated Service
summary: Geiger explains enlisting in the merchant marine because the National Maritime Union had desegregated the ships and rank mattered less than the job, the opposite of the segregated Navy where black sailors could only be cooks or waiters, then training as a radio radar officer and shipping out on a Pacific tanker through the battles at Ulithi and Leyte Gulf.

title: The SS Booker T. Washington
summary: Geiger describes grabbing an opening on the SS Booker T. Washington, the only ship in the American fleet with a black captain, Hugh Mulzac, who had held a master's license for years but never a command, and its integrated crew of black and white mates and engineers, on which he made several trips, some of them to Europe.

title: Integrate the White Waiting Room
summary: Geiger recounts how the integrated crew, sent to segregated Norfolk for repairs, would put on their dress uniforms and integrate the white waiting room of the railroad station, daring the police to arrest them, and would enrage ticket clerks by asking for tickets to the United States, his first nonviolent direct-action confrontation in the real world.

title: You Don't Become a Doctor
summary: Geiger tells how, floating around on the water, he decided he was interested in biology and medicine despite his father's repeated wish that he not become a doctor, and enrolled as a pre-med at the University of Chicago because he wanted what he saw as a real education.

title: Sit Down and Refuse to Leave
topic: CORE and the AVC in Chicago
summary: Geiger describes his Chicago activism with CORE, walking interracial crews into restaurants that would not serve blacks and inviting the other diners to refuse service too, and his role as civil-liberties chairman of the campus chapter of the American Veterans Committee, all while working night shifts at the International News Service.

title: Robbed Their Files Blind
summary: Geiger recounts the best-documented campaign of his life, after a faculty member's black servant was refused care: discovering the University of Chicago's lying-in hospital barred black patients while others diverted them to Provident, gathering sixty pages of documentation from veterans' secretary wives, finding admissions minutes that a qualified black applicant would not be admitted at this time, and chancellor Robert Maynard Hutchins calling it appalling but doing nothing.

title: Follow the Money
topic: The Howard Plan and the Strike
summary: Geiger tells how he arranged with Dr. W. Montague Cobb of Howard for strong black applicants to also apply to Chicago, each secretly paired with a white veteran of matching grades and MCAT scores, culminating in a student-faculty strike on December 7, 1947, and how, with the university still refusing, he took the documentation to a Pittsburgh foundation, after which it called to ask what they wanted, a lesson in following the money before Watergate.

title: The Kiss of Death Letter
summary: Geiger describes being called to Howard by Dr. W. Montague Cobb, who showed him an American Medical Association letter sent to every dean flagging his extracurricular activities, the kiss of death, and offering him a place at Howard, which Geiger declined, unwilling to take another student's place and unsure medicine was what he wanted.

title: Science Editor at the Wire Service
summary: Geiger recounts becoming science and medicine editor at the International News Service for about four years, interviewing Nobel laureates and covering major research, an education in medicine from a different vantage point, while he and his first wife Mary informally adopted her preteen brother and sister out of a broken home.

title: A Class of Bent Arrows
summary: Geiger describes assigning himself to cover the deans' meeting on the Willie Sutton principle and being recruited to Western Reserve's radical new integrated curriculum by one-man admissions dean Jack McCoy, who each year admitted a dozen bent arrows, deviants like Geiger, into his preceptor group alongside women and African American students unusual for the time.

title: An Older Student in Cleveland
summary: Geiger reflects on the advantage of starting medical school at almost thirty after a life as an activist, merchant seaman, and journalist, unlike classmates who knew little beyond school, and on the usual wonders and uncertainties of the immersive early years and whether medicine was really what he wanted.

title: Medicine Is Politics Writ Large
summary: Geiger recounts the second-year epiphany on the medical-school steps, looking out at Cleveland and realizing that who got sick was a social phenomenon, thinking he had invented social medicine only to find the Germans and British had written it a century earlier, and embracing Virchow's idea that medicine is simply politics writ large.

title: Flying to New York for Freelance Work
summary: Geiger describes mentors Margaret Mead and Barry Commoner, a committee on the social responsibility of science at the AAAS where he met Rockefeller Foundation vice president Warren Weaver, and using Western Reserve's free Tuesdays to fly to New York for freelance writing assignments to support his family before flying back for Wednesday classes.

part: South Africa and the Community Health Center
title: A Letter to Warren Weaver
summary: Geiger tells how he turned down Warren Weaver's offer to be the Rockefeller Foundation's science editor, explaining his social-medicine epiphany in a letter, and how Weaver, knowing the foundation funded Sidney and Emily Kark's pioneering community health centers in apartheid South Africa, sent him reprints showing a place that was actually practicing what he wanted to do.

part: South Africa and the Community Health Center
title: Three Places Had to Say Yes
summary: Geiger recounts his first exercise in grantsmanship, needing Western Reserve, Sidney Kark's University of Natal Medical School in Durban, and the Rockefeller Foundation each to say yes, and writing each that the other two already had, securing funding to go before 1960 and the full-scale ideological apartheid of Verwoerd.

part: South Africa and the Community Health Center
title: A Window of Opportunity
summary: Geiger describes the window before full apartheid when South Africa flirted with a national health service, the Karks and other medical students taking over a clinic in overcrowded Alexandra Township to build arguably the first community health center, and Sidney Kark's systematic study documenting appalling morbidity and mortality among African children.

part: South Africa and the Community Health Center
title: Pholela and Lamontville
summary: Geiger recounts his four and a half months in 1958 at the rural Pholela health center and the Zulu housing project at Lamontville near Durban, meeting people from the African National Congress and the Indian National Congress, and learning what it meant to assume total responsibility for a community's health, housing, food, water, and disease.

part: South Africa and the Community Health Center
title: The Social Pathology of Syphilis
summary: Geiger describes Sidney Kark's paper The Social Pathology of Syphilis, tracing the disease at Pholela to apartheid's migrant-labor system that sent working-age men away on eleven-month contracts, and the center's use of indigenous community health workers, a community vegetable garden, and skim-milk distribution that the government called communism.

part: Boston, Harvard, and the Road to Mississippi
title: A Teaching Hospital for Poor People
summary: Geiger recounts returning intending to do international health, doing his medical residency on the Harvard service at Boston City Hospital because it cared for poor people, and taking more than two years off for an NIH fellowship in social sciences in medicine at Harvard, then organizing in Boston for the 1963 March on Washington.

part: Boston, Harvard, and the Road to Mississippi
title: The Medical Committee for Human Rights
summary: Geiger notes Truman's executive order integrating the armed forces under the pressure of a second threatened march, then describes finishing his residency, taking a junior faculty post at the Harvard School of Public Health in 1964, and almost immediately taking a leave of absence because that summer brought the formation of the Medical Committee for Human Rights.

title: Because White Lives Were Valuable
summary: Geiger describes being asked to be the August field coordinator for the Medical Committee for Human Rights in Jackson, his misgiving at the irony that the summer project would work because the nation valued the white volunteers' lives, and his sense that the murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner had registered in the national consciousness.

title: How Can You Be Sleeping
summary: Geiger recounts flying to Jackson to a full-scale MCHR operation, a rotating crew fanning out to support the freedom schools and voter registration under COFO, meeting Aaron Henry of the NAACP, and contrasting volunteers who documented brutal conditions reminiscent of South Africa with a smaller for-show group, recalling a doctor in a motel room scolding his wife for sleeping while he was down there freedom fighting.

title: The First Protest Against Tuskegee
summary: Geiger describes realizing in Hinds County that he need not go abroad to find such conditions, and recruiting Dr. Count D. Gibson Jr., chairman of preventive medicine at Tufts and a Georgia native, relaying that historian Susan Reverby later found the first protest letter against the Tuskegee syphilis study was written by Gibson, a study in which black sharecroppers were denied penicillin to study a natural history already published in Scandinavia.

title: The COFO Creep
summary: Geiger recounts how Count Gibson's southern accent deepened the moment he returned to Mississippi, and the COFO creep, the trick of barely slowing at railroad crossings so as not to reveal you were an outsider by actually stopping, with Gibson's head shoved out the car window to answer the police in his accent whenever they were stopped.

part: The Idea of a Delta Health Center
title: What Do We Do Now
summary: Geiger describes returning to his Harvard job while a national MCHR was organized, becoming national field coordinator, going back to Mississippi on weekends, the autumn malaise of what to do after Freedom Summer, and the December meeting the Delta Ministry organized in Greenville of leftover summer people, indigenous and from outside.

part: The Idea of a Delta Health Center
title: The Headache That Went Away
summary: Geiger recounts starting the small free Winnick Clinic in Holmes County under MCHR with money from Luke and Ruth Wilson and support from Dr. Robert Smith and psychiatrist Dr. Alvin Poussaint, and the Greenville meeting floundering until his headache lifted as he remembered Pholela, proposing that a northern medical school build a comprehensive community health center, an idea others enriched with community involvement and community control though it stayed abstract.

title: Let's Talk About the Deal
summary: Geiger recounts being grounded by fog in Atlanta with Count Gibson, who proposed that if Geiger found the money Tufts would sponsor the project, turning it into a real plan aimed at the Office of Economic Opportunity and its principle of maximum feasible participation, and approaching William Kissick of the administration's Appalachian initiative.

title: Take 300,000 and Do It Now
summary: Geiger describes Kissick passing him to Elizabeth Shore at OEO as a wild man with a crazy idea, a January 1965 meeting where he spent two and a half hours laying out the community health center concept and its South African models to Sanford Kravitz, and his timid request for a thirty-thousand-dollar feasibility study met with Kravitz telling him to take three hundred thousand and do it now, against a background of devastating unattended illness found in Head Start and the Job Corps.

title: A Budget for 1.2 Million
summary: Geiger describes writing the first grant application for two community health centers, a southern rural one and one at the Columbia Point housing project in House Speaker John McCormack's district, returning with a budget grown to one-point-two million dollars, and his strategy against the southern governors' veto: listing the project as Southern rural across ten states so the site stayed secret and out of any single delegation's reach.

title: Don't Steal Our Patients
summary: Geiger describes Shriver's reservations about entering health and Mississippi, the public-health establishment's resistance embodied by Boston's George White Health Centers whose slogan was no diagnoses made and no prescriptions written, the opening of Columbia Point, scouting near Sparta, Georgia, and a call from Dr. John Hatch of the Boston Housing Authority, who had seen the grant and asked whether the southern positions were filled.

title: Half the Class Sat Beside Him
summary: Geiger calls John Hatch the most brilliant community organizer he ever met, recounting how Hatch sued a Kentucky law school that admitted only whites and was told to sit by himself until half the white class sat beside him, then took a social-work degree at Atlanta University and, hired instantly, became the first of the group to scout Sparta, where he stood at the country club's back door to get food.

title: A Physician Beside Dr. King
summary: Geiger recounts flying to Selma after the Edmund Pettus Bridge, an MCHR physician needing to be beside Dr. King at all times because someone would try to kill him, flying with King to Montgomery and riding with him to find a place to stay, and sitting in the corner that night for the long heated discussion among King, the young James Forman, and SNCC.

title: Alabama's Good Samaritan Law
topic: The March Argument and an Arrest
summary: Geiger describes the tense night over whether to defy the injunction against marching to Montgomery and SNCC's resentment of King, the national outpouring stirred further by the murder of Reverend James Reeb, and being arrested with Dr. Richard Hausknecht and hauled before the Alabama medical society, citing the state's Good Samaritan law, then meeting John Lewis and arranging to send him and Ivanhoe Donaldson to Boston for the skull fracture from the bridge.

title: Concrete Shells in an All-Black Town
summary: Geiger describes the OEO ban on bricks and mortar that it would relax for renovation, hearing of unfinished concrete buildings in Mound Bayou meant for the JC Campbell Junior College, and discovering an all-black town with its own government where people could vote, sheltering an integrated crew, with a forty-bed hospital run by the Knights and Daughters of Tabor that had grown from selling burial insurance.

title: The Dean Sitting In
summary: Geiger recounts the JC Campbell shells tied up in a Jackson receivership, proposing Mound Bayou to OEO while keeping the grant out of Mississippi amid Senator Stennis's attack on the Child Development Group of Mississippi Head Start, the opening of Columbia Point on December 11, 1965, and timing a day Shriver testified on the Hill to walk past the secretary and sit in his office, an observer noting it was the first time in the sixties that the dean was sitting in.

title: I Type a Hundred Words a Minute
summary: Geiger recounts Shriver handing them to Julius Richmond, the deputy director, with whom he worked out an agreement by evening and, rather than wait for secretaries, typed it himself at a hundred words a minute, after which Richmond took it up to Shriver, who signed it and sent it back with a congratulatory note and a gift of raspberries, leaving them free for Mississippi.

title: A Decoy in Batesville
summary: Geiger recounts convincing OEO that prefab modules built by the same firm working in Watts would count as renovation under a lease-purchase, then conspicuously scouting Batesville as a decoy while giving the grant to Joe Disparti, who passed it through a Batesville doctor so it landed on the governor's desk, recapitulating Pholela and Lamontville in the Delta.

title: Three Oranges Came Up
summary: Geiger describes the governor and Stennis screaming at Shriver, a roar from the state medical society and health commissioner Archie Gray, and his realization that nothing barred a grant to a Massachusetts institution to act in Mississippi, leaving the project veto-proof, then meeting the Mississippi State Medical Society at an AMA meeting where, after their insistence that care was not needed in Batesville, he named Mound Bayou and watched three oranges come up as they urged him toward the town he had wanted all along.

title: I Picked Cotton for Three Weeks
summary: Geiger describes John Hatch disappearing for three weeks scouting Bolivar County, returning to say he had picked cotton and lived in sharecropper shacks, knowing the leaders and the needs, and the profound destitution of the Delta as mechanized agriculture collapsed the sharecropper system, one cotton-picking machine replacing a hundred croppers.

title: Thirty Doctors in the Men's Room
summary: Geiger recounts months addressing the Delta and Bolivar County medical societies, speaking as a doctor rather than accusing, a fifty-to-one vote to disapprove at the Greenwood country club with fifty abstentions, and thirty doctors following him into the men's room at the dinner afterward only to quietly tell him to go ahead, his first lesson that white professionals of goodwill would help if never publicly exposed, with Shriver's spy later feeding lies about him.

title: The Prisoner's Dock
summary: Geiger recounts being placed in the prisoner's dock at the Bolivar County Medical Society meeting, the difficulty of recruiting to a village of two thousand where two failing black fraternal-order hospitals could not meet payroll, renting the old three-story mansion once occupied by Dr. T.R.M. Howard to cover their payroll, and writing a grant with consultant George Allen to merge the Knights and Daughters of Tabor and Sarah Brown hospitals into a community hospital for all the poor of Bolivar County.

title: Community Participation, Not Control
summary: Geiger describes the merged hospital never becoming a true community hospital because the fraternal orders kept control and continued selling insurance amid conflicts of interest, while John Hatch patiently organized ten local health associations across the five hundred square miles of northern Bolivar County, home to twelve to fourteen thousand black people, using the black Baptist church as an organizational model.

title: A Shield Against Opposition
summary: Geiger recounts recruiting a remarkable staff including Catholic nurse-midwife Sister Mary Stella, nurse Chris Hansen, Joe Disparti, and Phyllis Cunningham, and going to the Bolivar County sheriff to warn that as a former journalist he knew every newspaper in the country, so any harm to the integrated crew would make headlines like Selma, and the sheriff assured him there would be no difficulty.

title: Wells, Privies, and Outreach
summary: Geiger describes national press from the New York Times to Time Magazine attracting recruits and shielding the project, the community organization, surveys, and prenatal care underway before any clinic was built, and John Hatch's community health action division recruiting black sanitarian Andrew James from Ohio to dig wells and sanitary privies for people drinking from drainage ditches.

title: Fifteen, Then Twenty-Five, Then a Hundred
summary: Geiger recounts renting a vacant church parsonage in Mound Bayou with the living room as a waiting room and bedrooms as exam rooms, recruiting mostly pediatricians like Leon Kruger and Roy Brown for a population whose median age was fifteen, patient numbers jumping from fifteen the first day to a hundred by the third, families renting wide-body trailers to live in, and Dr. Aaron Shirley and Dr. Robert Smith driving up from Jackson two days a week to help.

title: Ted Kennedy's Fifty Million
summary: Geiger recounts Senator Edward Kennedy visiting Columbia Point in August 1966, taking up health as his issue, and writing legislation creating an office of health affairs within OEO with a fifty-million-dollar budget to build health centers, ending the research-and-demonstration phase and launching community health centers in a major way as congresspeople realized they could bring one home.

title: A Pearl Bailey With an MD
summary: Geiger describes how their care differed from the white practice of writing a prescription across a desk without examining the patient, and the recruitment of surgeon Harvey Sanders, physician Charles Humphrey, and Dr. Helen Barnes, a Mississippi-native obstetrician he chased for two years, along with clinical director David Weeks from Aramco, with the ten health associations sending delegates to the North Bolivar County Health Council.

title: Thousands Raised Their Hands
summary: Geiger recounts organizing as a charitable trust when the governor would not charter a nonprofit, John Hatch turning a plan for a hundred vegetable gardens into the North Bolivar County Farm Cooperative when thousands of families volunteered, L.C. Dorsey rising from a ninth-grade dropout to deputy director and earning her GED at the center, and the office of education sending local people to nursing, medical, and pharmacy schools, including the first two sent to Tufts who returned to work there.

title: The Jolly Black Giant
summary: Geiger recounts training the first ten registered black sanitarians in Mississippi history and a college-prep course feeding Stony Brook, and a Ford Foundation grant that grew the farm co-op into a five-hundred-acre partly mechanized operation involving thousands of Bolivar County people who traded labor for income and crop shares, raising tons of vegetables instead of cotton.

title: Palliative, Not Capital
summary: Geiger describes dreams of a cannery to grow and can soul food for northern markets with backing from the Jolly Green Giant company, the government refusing to grant them a defunct white-run cannery thirty miles away, making clear it favored palliative programs for black communities over capital programs and real entrepreneurship, and the health council shifting independently funded programs under its own control.

title: A Bus Network Across the Delta
summary: Geiger recounts the health council running an emergency food program, a library and cultural center, early-childhood intervention, a bus network across the ten towns that also became a route to jobs, and Mrs. Robinson in Round Lake organizing meals and company for isolated elderly people, framing it all as intervening in the social determinants of health, futile otherwise, like rehydrating an infant only to send the child back to drink from the same drainage ditch.

part: The Legacy of the Health Centers
title: A Map With Measles
summary: Geiger reflects that the work, involving hundreds of thousands of professionals, grew into more than twelve hundred community health centers at over eight thousand sites that look like measles on a map of the United States, serving more than twenty million low-income people, urban and rural, in housing projects, schools, and the migrant streams.

part: The Legacy of the Health Centers
title: Fifty-One Percent Patients
summary: Geiger explains that since 1975 every federally qualified community health center must by law be a nonprofit with an elected board at least fifty-one percent current patients, a patient voice unique in American health care, and describes his 1971 move to Stony Brook, where the health council made what he believes are the only reverse site visits by a community group, choosing it for its scholarships, sending nineteen Bolivar County people, while John Hatch became the first African American to hold an endowed chair at the University of North Carolina School of Public Health.

title: Training the Next Generation
summary: Geiger recounts leaving Stony Brook for the new City University of New York Medical School devoted to training minority and low-income students for primary care in underserved areas, admitting them out of high school into a combined program with eight community health centers as the clinical campus, where he founded a department and worked from 1978 until nominally retiring around 1996.

title: Still Trying to Disturb the Peace
summary: Geiger describes continuing work through national medical fellowships and a new Medical-Legal Partnerships organization that puts lawyers in health centers to address the housing and exploitation making patients sick, his hope at eighty-seven to help create a next generation with this orientation, and the interview closing with the Institute of Medicine's honor for creating the model of the contemporary community health center.
